observation_summary: The system failed to return the correct default value for primitive 'double' types, returning an integer '0' instead of a double '0.0D'. This caused ClassCastExceptions and assertion failures in multiple tests.
hypothesis: The 'primitiveValues' map in the 'Primitives' utility class was initialized with an incorrect value for the 'double.class' key. Specifically, it was assigned an integer '0' instead of a double literal '0D', leading to type mismatch errors when the framework attempted to cast the returned value.
prediction: The code will show an assignment of '0' to 'double.class' in the 'primitiveValues' map initialization, which will be corrected to '0D'.
experiment_rationale: The fix diff explicitly shows the change from 'primitiveValues.put(double.class, 0);' to 'primitiveValues.put(double.class, 0D);'. This confirms that the root cause was an incorrect value assignment during initialization.
reasoning_summary: The defect is a classic case of an incorrect value being assigned to a map during initialization. It does not involve complex control flow logic (Algorithm/Method), missing guards (Checking), or structural design changes (Function/Class/Object). The fix is a simple correction of a constant value.

alternative_types.why_not_primary: The fix is not a change in procedural logic or computation, but a correction of a static value assigned to a map.
There is no missing conditional check or guard; the issue is that the value provided by the map is of the wrong type.
